_note: Production Design projects. Each entry renders the card (production-design.html Projects view) AND the full project page (project.html?slug=…). Images: give `src` (a path under assets/work/<slug>/) once filed; leave it out to show a labelled placeholder. Managed by the /update-site skill — safe to hand-edit too.

projects.title: How Can I Not Forget
projects.logline: In a near-future Los Angeles where traumatic memories can be erased, a grieving woman begins to remember the massacre — and the truth about her mother's death — someone she loves has been trying to make her forget.
diary: The design projects Nora's internal state onto physical space, and keeps pressing the question of what's real. The apartment is high-class and a little too clean; the hospital has no trace of humanity in it — both built on a rigid, structural order, the kind a world keeps when it's hiding something.
The surgery room is the piece I keep returning to: its clear curtain drape is the whole film in one object — something you see through and still can't see the truth behind.

items.label: still
items.alt: How Can I Not Forget — ORION search prop
items.description: ORION is the film’s near-future search engine — the web on Nora’s laptop. When she looks up the massacre and the riot, the results come back filtered: an overview that explains them away, and a notice that results were “excluded pending verification.” It’s how the state quietly erases what happened. The search, results, and articles are all built and clickable.

projects.title: Ghost MV
projects.logline: A music video about remembering — the ghost of someone gone, somehow still here.

projects.title: Sonata
projects.logline: Two Korean-American sisters — one at the piano, one at the violin — circle a childhood duet they could never play together, until their mother's illness finally brings them to the same song.
diary: Sonata takes place in one Korean-American family home, and the design leans all the way into its clutter — the lived-in mess of a house three people have filled over years. I dressed it full: stacked mail and sheet music, medicine and tea sets, framed certificates and small violin trophies on every ledge, laundry drying on a rack in the middle of the living room. The mess isn't background; it holds the tangle between a mother and her two daughters, and the piano they all circle sits at the center of it.

projects.title: Soup 湯
projects.logline: A grieving woman running her family's three-generation Chinese restaurant is offered Meng Po's soup — one sip to forget everything and be reborn — until a young apprentice gives her a reason to hold on instead.
diary: The restaurant in the script is a small, worn room — the kind a family keeps for three generations. What we found was the opposite: too big, too open, nothing that felt lived in. So we made it smaller. We built a wall.

The work split cleanly. I designed the structure — the beams and supports that had to stand on their own on set. Ariel, our production designer, designed the look and led the texturing: layer over layer until fresh drywall read as years, a plexiglass window cut in, graphics and props dressed across it until the seam was gone. By the end, the wall had always been there.

credits.name: Adrianne Meave, Eri Wong, Varshini Satoor, Ying (Diana) Zhang, Yining (Coco) Zhong, Yunlei Liu

projects.title: Antidote
projects.logline: A feverish boy and a world-weary stranger share a hospital ward, where a tin of mints — passed off as painkillers — becomes a quiet mercy that keeps a family from breaking apart.
diary: Antidote unfolds in a single hospital ward — a sick boy and a worn-out stranger sharing a room while the boy's parents carry in the weight of the world outside. I designed the costumes to hold that split. The boy and the old man wear grounded, human color — warm reds and soft, desaturated earth tones, the palette of skin and of being cared for.

The parents were the variable. Their clothes shift with their state of mind across the film — cooling toward something harder and more capitalistic when the money takes hold of them, then warming and lightening in the moments they stop being a provider and a caretaker and become, again, just a mother and a father.

The most deliberate piece was the jacket. People shed a jacket or pull one on as they enter or leave a room, but the gesture is also armor — putting it on raises a guard against the outside, taking it off lets it fall. And with nowhere to source a child's hospital gown, I made one: cutting an adult gown apart and sewing it back down to a boy's size.

credits.name: Arwen Wang, Sifan Liu, Yining Dai

projects.title: On the River Bank
projects.logline: A dance-driven music video that follows Mia through a mist-soaked forest as she chases an illusory success — past a breakfast of empty dishes and scattered poppies, a grove of mirrors hung from the trees, and a path of unreachable lamps — until she reaches the riverbank, and the selves she gathered along the way.
diary: The director set four symbols — flower, mirror, river, chain — and the whole world was built from dressed, found objects in an LA creek bed: a full breakfast table laid with empty china and scattered poppies, salvaged mirrors and lampshades staked through the trees, an ankle shackle, and a gradient-brown gown that bleeds toward red as Mia comes apart.

credits.name: Charlotte Hou, Eri Wong, Yilong Yang
